project_overview.project_development_objective: To facilitate the expansion of electricity supply of hydropower in Pakistan by installing a 2,160 MW hydropower plant on the main Indus River (expandable to 4,320 MW in future at very low cost), while improving access to socio-economic services for local communities in the project area and building WAPDA's capacity to prepare future hydropower projects
project_overview.total_financing: US$4,247.7 million (total project cost); IDA Credit: US$588.4 million (SDR 379.7 million); IDA Guarantee: US$460 million
project_overview.implementation_period: June 10, 2014 to December 31, 2021 (construction); Expected Closing Date: June 30, 2022
project_overview.beneficiaries: Millions of energy users (industry, households, farmers) nationwide; local communities in Upper Kohistan District; WAPDA institutional capacity

primary_locations: Upper Kohistan District, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province
Dasu Town (8 km from project site)
Transmission line corridor: Dasu to Islamabad via Mansehra (250 km new line, 100 km rehabilitation)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: national (electricity supply); regional (project implementation in Upper Kohistan)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Upper Kohistan District is in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province which has experienced security challenges; document notes 'persistence of conflict in the border areas and security challenges throughout the country'; security situation assessed and plan prepared to address these issues in SRMP

key_components.component_name: Component A: Construction of Main Hydraulic Structure on Indus River
key_components.description: Civil works for main dam structure (RCC), spillway, 9 Low Level Outlets (LLOs), 2 flushing tunnels, river diversion works. Maximum height 242m above foundation, crest length 570m, operational storage capacity 0.82 BCM
key_components.financing: US$1,479.7 million (IDA: US$10.0 million; WAPDA: US$55.0 million; Commercial: US$1,257.8 million; Export Credits: US$157.0 million)

key_components.description: B1: Construction of 4 headrace tunnels, 4 tailrace tunnels, underground powerhouse, underground substations (for DHP-I: 2 HTs, 2 TTs, powerhouse for 6 units of 360 MW each). B2: Supply and installation of 6 turbines, generators, electro-mechanical equipment (360 MW units)
key_components.financing: US$1,397.8 million (IDA: US$0 million; WAPDA: US$109.3 million; Commercial: US$642.1 million; Export Credits: US$546.0 million; AF: US$100.4 million)

key_components.description: Access roads, Karakoram Highway (KKH) relocation (62 km new KKH at higher elevation), 132 kV transmission line from Dubair to Dasu, offices, on-site housing, construction camps, possibly access tunnel to powerhouse
key_components.financing: US$344.8 million (IDA: US$183.9 million; WAPDA: US$160.9 million)

key_components.description: D1: Double circuit 500 kV transmission line from Dasu to Islamabad via Mansehra (250 km new line, 100 km rehabilitation of existing lines). D2: Design, social/environmental assessments, management plans, construction supervision. D3: Social and environmental management costs. Implemented by NTDC
key_components.financing: US$350.0 million (IDA: US$15.0 million; WAPDA: US$0 million; NTDC: US$80.0 million; AF: US$255.0 million)

key_components.component_name: Component E: Implementation of Social and Environmental Management Plans, and Glacial, Sediment River Monitoring
key_components.description: E1: Social and Resettlement Management Plan (SRMP) - compensation, resettlement, livelihood restoration, gender programs, public health, local area development (US$438.9M). E2: Environmental Management Plan (EMP) - ecological conservation, fisheries, forestry management, monitoring (US$54.5M). E3: Flood warning system, watershed, sediment and river monitoring, glacial monitoring (US$10.5M)
key_components.financing: US$503.9 million (IDA: US$266.5 million; WAPDA: US$237.4 million)

key_components.component_name: Component F: Construction Supervision, Monitoring and Evaluation
key_components.description: F1: Construction Supervision Consultants (CSCs) for supervision, contract management, quality control, certification of payments, financial management (US$91.1M). F2: Project Management and M&E Consultants for monitoring project impacts, EMP and SRMP implementation (US$8.0M)
key_components.financing: US$99.1 million (IDA: US$53.0 million; WAPDA: US$25.0 million; AF: US$21.1 million)

key_components.component_name: Component G: Project Management Support, Capacity Building, Technical Assistance, Training, and Future Project Preparation
key_components.description: G1: PMU support, incremental staff salaries, operational costs, audits (US$42.4M). G2: WAPDA capacity building, Independent Panel of Experts, technical assistance in dam engineering, O&M, financing strategies (US$10.0M). G3: Strategic studies, pilot projects, preparation of future Indus Cascade projects (US$20.0M)
key_components.financing: US$72.4 million (IDA: US$60.0 million; WAPDA: US$12.4 million)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Water and Power Development Authority (WAPDA) for main project components (A, B, C, E, F, G); National Transmission and Dispatch Company Limited (NTDC) for transmission line (Component D); General Manager Dasu HPP serves as Project Director reporting to Member (Water); Financial management responsibility with GM Finance (Power) under Member (Finance)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by Secretary, Ministry of Water and Power with members from Planning, Finance, EAD, WAPDA, NTDC, NHA, KP provincial/district administration; Project Advisory Committee at site with WAPDA, District Administration, local communities; Independent Panel of Experts (IPOE) for technical, environmental, social oversight; Environment and Social Management Unit (ESMU) within PMU; Grievance Redress Committees (GRCs) at 4 tiers (village, union council, district, project level)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: International Competitive Bidding (ICB) for major contracts (main structure, power tunnels/powerhouse, equipment, transmission line); National Competitive Bidding (NCB) for contracts under US$6 million; Shopping procedures for contracts under US$100,000; Community-based contracting for minor works; Pre-qualification mandatory for contracts over US$10 million; Slice and package method allowing bidders to bid on one or both major contracts; Contractors expected to arrange commercial financing with IDA Guarantee support
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Quarterly reports by Project Directors within 15 days of quarter end; Annual reports by September 30 each year; Semi-annual Bank supervision missions; Mid-term reviews by October 31, 2016 and October 2019; Implementation Completion Report within 6 months of closing; Independent M&E Consultants for continuous monitoring of project impacts and EMP/SRMP implementation; Third-party monitoring on semi-annual basis; Internal audits semi-annually by WAPDA; External audits annually by independent auditors; Detailed monitoring plan for environmental and social parameters (water quality, air quality, noise, wildlife, fish, dam safety, etc.)

identified_risks.risk_description: Persistence of conflict in border areas and security challenges throughout Pakistan affecting all aspects of life; security situation in Upper Kohistan District; potential for conflict between local communities and migrant construction workers; influx of approximately 5,000 construction workers and their families
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Security situation assessed and plan prepared in SRMP volume on 'Hydropower Development, Conflict and Security Issues'; Migration Management Plan; Awareness campaigns; Code of conduct for workers; Grievance mechanisms; Involvement of local administration and tribal leaders (maliks, jirgas); Community-based approach to resettlement

identified_risks.risk_description: Internal accountability in WAPDA remains weak; little accountability or transparency in decision-making; size of contracts (up to US$1.5 billion) presents procurement oversight challenges; potential for corruption and fraud in large contracts; staff turnover risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Governance and Accountability Action Plan (GAAP); Independent Panel of Experts (IPOE) to review prequalification and bid evaluation for major contracts; Dedicated Environment/Social Officers at site; Certification of no-conflict-of-interest by PMU members, evaluation committee, bidders; Procurement website with transparent documentation; Complaint handling system; Enhanced Bank supervision with quarterly reviews for first 18 months; Requirement to minimize staff turnover; Central Contract Cell of WAPDA review; Public disclosure of procurement information

identified_risks.risk_description: Resettlement of 767 households (6,953 persons) from 34 villages; loss of 4,643 ha of land including 425 ha agricultural land; relocation of 945 houses, 197 shops, 31 mosques, 7 schools, 17 graveyards; impacts on livelihoods and cultural practices; limited impact on household income (estimated 5% average) but variable across households; potential gender and vulnerable group impacts; influx of construction workers may create social tensions
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Social and Resettlement Management Plan (SRMP) with multiple volumes; Multi-option resettlement approach respecting tribal structures and annual migration patterns; Compensation based on recent hydropower projects (Keyal Khawr, Diamer-Basha) with escalation; Livelihood restoration program with short-term and long-term support; Gender Action Plan addressing women's limited access to education and public participation; Public Health Action Plan; Area Development Fund of US$30 million for local development; Grievance Redress Committees at 4 tiers; Independent monitoring of SRMP implementation; Benefit-sharing initiatives to be explored; Extensive community consultations through jirgas and participatory methods

identified_risks.risk_description: Loss of 1,800 ha terrestrial habitat including 1.6% of Kaigah Community Conservation Area (critical habitat with endangered Markhor sheep); submergence of 571 ha aquatic habitat; barrier effect to fish migration; reduced flows in 4.4 km dewatered reach during low flow season; sedimentation impacts (27% of annual sediment flow trapped); impacts on 50,000+ rock carvings (Shatial) from future Diamer-Basha project; impacts on migratory birds (Indus Flyway); transmission line may cross environmentally sensitive Palas Valley (IBA); climate change impacts on glacial melt and hydrology
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Environmental and Social Assessment (ESA) and Environmental Management Plan (EMP); Ecological flow commitment of 20 m3/s from dam plus 222 m3/s from tailrace (total 242 m3/s downstream); Fish hatchery for snow carp restocking; Community-based conservation areas in at least 2 sites (Kandia, Laachi, Sazin Kot, Shori) to offset critical habitat loss; Forest Rejuvenation Plan for high altitude forests; Afforestation and tree plantation (105,000 trees); Protection of Shatial rock carvings with museum and tourism facilities; Avian Risk Assessment for transmission line; Alternative routing studies for transmission line to avoid Palas Valley; Monitoring of downstream water quality, aquatic habitats, fish populations; Sediment management through flushing after 15 years; Climate change assessment and glacier monitoring; Environmental Codes of Practice (18 ECPs) for contractors; Third-party environmental monitoring

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of commercial financing shortfall; market conditions may not support anticipated commercial financing levels (base case assumes 56% commercial financing); commercial lenders require substantial credit enhancement; IDA Guarantee availability limited to US$460 million per cycle; potential for unfavorable financing terms; currency and interest rate risks; WAPDA's limited ability to raise commercial financing on its own balance sheet due to country risk perceptions
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Sequenced financing approach matching phased construction (reduces carrying costs and manages risk); IDA Guarantees (US$460 million per cycle) to enhance commercial financing; Export Credit Agency (ECA) coverage for equipment; Potential MIGA insurance for political risks; Alternative financing scenarios developed (Scenario B: 50% commercial for works, 85% for equipment; Scenario C: no commercial for works, 50% for equipment); Contingency measures if commercial financing unavailable: (i) reduce plant size to 1,080 MW (ERR still 21%), (ii) IFC financing for transmission line, (iii) additional IDA financing from IDA17/18; Market soundings with commercial banks and ECAs; Roadshow to financial centers; Contractor financing mobilization as part of bidding process; Put option structures for loan refinancing; Securitization of WAPDA assets considered; Escrow Account mechanism (similar to Tarbela IV) to ensure WAPDA payment capacity

identified_risks.risk_description: Large-scale construction in remote mountainous terrain (Upper Kohistan); complex underground powerhouse design; high seismic activity (Serious Seismic Danger Zone, Class VIII); landslide risks; glacial lake outburst flood (GLOF) risks; sedimentation management; construction schedule delays; cost overruns; coordination challenges with multiple large contracts (up to US$1.5 billion each)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Designs prepared by highly competent international consultants (DHC joint venture); Compliance with ICOLD guidelines for seismic design (Class VIII); Independent Panel of Experts (5 renowned international experts) reviewing designs and construction; State-of-art engineering modeling for dam design, spillway, outlets; Detailed site investigations and studies; Flood telemetry network for early warning system; Probable Maximum Flood (PMF) design considering GLOFs and extreme rainfall; Sediment management plan with flushing tunnels and LLOs; Large contract packaging to attract competent international contractors; Construction Supervision Consultants (CSCs) for quality control and supervision; Resident Engineers at site; Emergency Preparedness Plan; Dam Safety Organization annual surveillance and inspection; Instrumentation plan for dam safety monitoring; Detailed O&M and Emergency Preparedness Plans to be prepared 6-12 months before impoundment

identified_risks.risk_description: WAPDA's limited experience with large commercial financing; weak internal accountability and transparency; capacity constraints in environmental and social management; staff turnover risks; need for significant institutional strengthening
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive capacity building program under Component G (US$10 million for WAPDA strengthening); Technical assistance in dam engineering, O&M, financing strategies; Training programs for staff; Recruitment of expert staff under PMUs; Retention of international Construction Supervision Consultants; Project Management Support and M&E Consultants; Strengthening of WAPDA Environmental Cell (WEC) with GIS/MIS facility (US$0.3 million); Requirement to minimize staff turnover; Governance and Accountability Action Plan (GAAP) implementation; Capacity building and training budget of US$0.6 million; Institutional strengthening of WEC for future hydropower projects

identified_risks.risk_description: Large project with complex financial flows; multiple financing sources; commercial financing with different terms; potential for weak internal controls; cash flow management challenges; circular debt issues in power sector affecting WAPDA's payment capacity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Designated Account (DA) in US Dollars at National Bank of Pakistan; Advance and Direct Payment disbursement methods; Quarterly Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) within 45 days of quarter end; Annual audited financial statements within 6 months of fiscal year end; Internal audit by WAPDA semi-annually; External audit by Chartered Accountants annually; Financial Management Specialist in PMU; Prior review of payments over US$50,000 for land acquisition and resettlement; Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for compensation payments; Escrow Account mechanism (similar to Tarbela IV) to ensure WAPDA receives timely payments from CPPA/NTDC; Clearing of circular debt by government (PKR 480 billion cleared in June 2013); Escrow Account operational since 2013 ensuring 2-month billing buffer

identified_risks.risk_description: Large contracts (up to US$1.5 billion) present significant procurement oversight challenges; potential for corruption, collusion, fraud; complex bidding process with financing component; contractor selection based on financing capacity; limited competition in remote location
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: International Competitive Bidding (ICB) for all major contracts; Pre-qualification mandatory for contracts over US$10 million; Independent Panel of Experts review of prequalification and bid evaluation for major contracts; Procurement website with transparent documentation; Complaint handling system; Public opening of bids in presence of CSOs; Certification of no-conflict-of-interest by bidders; Disclosure of bidder agents and connections; Slice and package method for major contracts; Expected participation of 5-6 major international contractors; Central Contract Cell of WAPDA review; Bank prior review of all ICB contracts; Post-review of NCB contracts; Quarterly reviews for first 18 months; Procurement training for PMU staff; Detailed procurement plan and mapping of procurement process

identified_risks.risk_description: Cumulative impacts with future Diamer-Basha (4,500 MW) and other Indus Cascade projects; loss of 20,000+ rock carvings from DB project; changes in sediment transport affecting downstream ecology; impacts on migratory birds; transmission line crossing sensitive areas; climate change impacts on glacier melt and water availability; potential for 50% reduction in inflow by 2040 under worst-case climate scenarios
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA) prepared as part of ESA; Strategic Sector Environmental and Social Assessment (SSESA) of Indus Basin underway through WCAP; Adaptive management approach for sediment management; Coordination with future DB project on sediment flushing; Monitoring of downstream water quality and fish populations; Climate change assessment study; Glacier monitoring program; Flood telemetry network; Coordination of transmission line design with environmental sensitivity; Alternative routing studies for transmission line; Avian Risk Assessment; Community-based conservation programs; Forest management plans; Ecological monitoring program

identified_risks.risk_description: Coordination required with IMF Extended Fund Facility (EFF) program; alignment with Power Sector Reform Development Policy Credits; coordination with ADB and JICA; potential for IFC involvement in transmission line financing; MIGA insurance considerations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close coordination with IMF on power sector reforms; Parallel financing by ADB and JICA for Power Sector Reform DPCs; Coordination with IFC on transmission line financing options; Discussions with MIGA on political risk insurance; World Bank Group coordination through Transformational Power Initiative; Regular donor coordination meetings

identified_risks.risk_description: Difficulty accessing remote project site for monitoring; long implementation period (7+ years); sustainability of O&M after project completion; tariff sustainability; WAPDA's ability to maintain and operate the facility; potential for sediment accumulation reducing project life
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Independent M&E Consultants for continuous monitoring; Third-party monitoring on semi-annual basis; Regular Bank supervision missions (semi-annual, more frequent in early years); In-country consultant for regular site visits; Detailed monitoring plan with specific indicators and frequency; O&M Plan to be prepared 6 months before impoundment; Emergency Preparedness Plan; Dam Safety Organization annual surveillance; Tariff determined by NEPRA on cost-plus basis ensuring financial viability; WAPDA's good track record with existing hydropower plants; Sediment management plan with flushing facilities; Estimated project life over 50 years; Escrow Account mechanism ensuring payment capacity

identified_risks.risk_description: Resettlement of 767 households (6,953 persons); potential for inadequate compensation or livelihood restoration; cultural disruption from relocation to higher elevations; impacts on seasonal migration patterns; potential for conflict over resettlement site selection
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Social and Resettlement Management Plan (SRMP) with detailed resettlement framework; Multi-option resettlement approach respecting tribal structures; Compensation based on replacement cost using recent comparable projects; Livelihood restoration program with short-term and long-term support; Resettlement sites identified through community consultations (27 potential sites); Relocation within existing tribal territories and annual migration range; Grievance Redress Committees at 4 tiers; Independent monitoring of SRMP implementation; Area Development Fund (US$30 million) for local development; Benefit-sharing initiatives; Gender Action Plan; Public Health Action Plan; Extensive community consultations through jirgas

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: A - Full Assessment (significant environmental and social impacts)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Four-tier bottom-up Grievance Redress Committee (GRC) system: (i) Village Level GRC, (ii) Union Council Level GRC, (iii) District-Level GRC, (iv) Project Level Independent GRC chaired by retired civil judge. GRC members include affected community representatives selected through Jirga meetings, project staff, and local government representatives. Separate grievance redress cell established under Deputy Project Director. Procedures include filing, review, hearing, documentation, and notification of outcomes. Operation costs budgeted. Annual evaluation of GRC activities. Orientation and training provided to GRC members. Complaints tracked and resolved within specified timelines.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender Action Plan developed based on gender assessment. Key findings: women completely absent from public life, limited access to education, strong resistance to women's empowerment. Three-prong approach: (i) educate project staff and construction forces on gender sensitivity, (ii) educate men (local leaders, religious leaders, husbands) on importance of women accessing project benefits, (iii) identify and use existing entry points for women's participation. Interventions include: awareness campaigns with tribal leaders and religious leaders; gender sensitization training for project staff; dissemination of project information to women; women as primary beneficiaries of health and education programs; livelihood activities maximizing women's existing 'home-based' income generating activities (agriculture, poultry, firewood collection); skill training and micro-credit; socio-psychological support during relocation; women's participation in community health and education programs. Livelihood development programs to include opportunities for women in agriculture, microcredit, livestock, small businesses.
vulnerable_groups: Women (particularly in patriarchal society with limited public participation)
Children and elderly people (safety hazards from increased traffic)
Downstream fishing communities (570 ha aquatic habitat impacts)
Communities in Kaigah Conservation Area (habitat loss)
Construction workers and their families (health and safety risks)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project acknowledges 'persistence of conflict in the border areas and security challenges throughout the country' affecting all aspects of life in Pakistan. Upper Kohistan District located in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province which has experienced security challenges. Security situation in project area assessed and plan prepared in SRMP volume on 'Hydropower Development, Conflict and Security Issues: A Perspective'. Mitigation measures include: Migration Management Plan to address potential conflicts between local communities and influx of 5,000+ construction workers; Awareness campaigns; Code of conduct for workers; Grievance mechanisms; Involvement of local tribal leaders (maliks) and jirgas in decision-making; Community-based resettlement approach respecting tribal structures; Extensive community consultations; Public participation mechanisms; Communications strategy for transparency and information dissemination. Project designed to respect local cultural norms and traditional governance systems (jirga system). Benefit-sharing initiatives being explored to build local support.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Pakistan faces significant economic, governance and security challenges. Key fragility factors: (i) Persistence of conflict in border areas and security challenges throughout country; (ii) Weak governance and accountability in institutions (WAPDA noted to have weak internal accountability and transparency); (iii) Circular debt crisis in power sector (accumulated arrears about 4% of GDP in FY12/13); (iv) High fiscal deficits (6-8.5% of GDP); (v) Weak tax collection; (vi) Limited private sector credit; (vii) External account pressures; (viii) Weak institutional capacity; (ix) Political instability (though first democratic-to-democratic transfer of power in May 2013 noted as positive). Upper Kohistan District ranks very low in socioeconomic development indicators (literacy, school enrollment among lowest in country). Project area characterized by: mountainous terrain, sparse population, ethnic/religious/tribal divisions, strong traditional tribal culture, limited government services, no formal land records system, limited health facilities, limited access to education.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security situation in Upper Kohistan District and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province assessed. Potential security risks include: (i) Influx of 5,000+ construction workers and families creating potential for conflict with local communities; (ii) Increased traffic on Karakoram Highway (KKH) - the only lifeline to northern areas; (iii) Construction activities in remote mountainous area; (iv) Potential for theft or sabotage of equipment/materials; (v) Broader security challenges in KP Province. Mitigation measures: Security plan prepared in SRMP; Migration Management Plan; Awareness campaigns; Code of conduct for workers; Grievance mechanisms; Involvement of local administration and tribal leaders; Community-based approach; Regular monitoring and supervision; Emergency Preparedness Plan; Coordination with local authorities.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Significant displacement planned. Details: (i) Resettlement of 767 households totaling 6,953 persons from 34 villages; (ii) Land acquisition of 4,643 hectares (including 425 ha agricultural land); (iii) Relocation of 945 houses, 197 shops, 31 mosques, 7 schools, 17 graveyards; (iv) Affected villages: 34 villages on both banks of Indus River in Upper Kohistan District. Resettlement approach: Multi-option approach respecting tribal structures and annual migration patterns; Relocation within existing tribal territories at higher elevations; Compensation based on replacement cost (using Keyal Khawr and Diamer-Basha precedents); Livelihood restoration program with short-term and long-term support; 27 potential resettlement sites identified through community consultations; Grievance Redress Committees; Independent monitoring; Area Development Fund (US$30 million); Benefit-sharing initiatives. Compensation: Land compensation budget US$243.3 million; Property/structure compensation US$42.8 million; Livelihood support US$21.3 million; Resettlement site development US$63.0 million. Implementation timeline: Land acquisition and resettlement in first 24 months of project (IDA funds limited to US$75 million for land, US$36 million for other compensation in first 24 months; WAPDA to finance from year 3 onwards). Extensive community consultations conducted (2,392 persons involved in various consultation meetings between April-October 2012; final round of consultations in February 2014).
